stem: Consider the ring \( R = \mathbb{Z}_{[[m]]} \times \mathbb{Z}_{[[n]]} \). What is the characteristic of \( R \)? (The characteristic is the least positive integer \( k \) with \( k r = 0 \) for every \( r \in R \).)
solution: In \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[m]]} \times \mathbb{Z}_{[[n]]} \) an element \( (x, y) \) satisfies \( k(x,y) = 0 \) for all \( (x,y) \) exactly when \( [[m]] \mid k \) and \( [[n]] \mid k \). The least such \( k \) is \( \operatorname{lcm}([[m]], [[n]]) = \dfrac{[[m]] \cdot [[n]]}{\gcd([[m]], [[n]])} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is \( [[m]] \cdot [[n]] \), the number of elements of \( R \); the characteristic is the lcm, which equals the product only when the moduli are coprime.
This adds the two moduli instead of taking their least common multiple.
This is \( \gcd([[m]], [[n]]) \); the characteristic is the least common multiple, not the greatest common divisor.
This is one more than the correct characteristic.
This is one fewer than the correct characteristic.
This is two more than the correct characteristic.

stem: Consider the polynomial ring \( R = \mathbb{Z}_{[[n]]}[x] \) (polynomials in \( x \) with coefficients in \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[n]]} \), such as \( 1 + x + x^{[[deg]]} \)). What is the characteristic of \( R \)?
solution: Adjoining an indeterminate \( x \) does not change how many times the multiplicative identity \( 1 \) must be added to reach \( 0 \): in \( R = \mathbb{Z}_{[[n]]}[x] \), \( k \cdot 1 = k \) (the constant polynomial) vanishes exactly when \( [[n]] \mid k \). So \( R \) has the same characteristic as its coefficient ring \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[n]]} \), namely \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is one less than \( n \); the characteristic is exactly \( n \), not \( n-1 \).
This is one more than \( n \); the characteristic is exactly \( n \), not \( n+1 \).
This mistakenly scales by the degree of the polynomial used as an example, which has no effect on the characteristic.
This uses the degree of the example polynomial instead of the modulus of the coefficient ring.
This is two more than the correct characteristic.
This is two fewer than the correct characteristic.

stem: Let \( R = M_{[[k]]}(\mathbb{Z}_{[[n]]}) \), the ring of \( [[k]] \times [[k]] \) matrices with entries in \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[n]]} \), under the usual matrix addition and multiplication. What is the characteristic of \( R \)?
solution: The characteristic of a matrix ring \( M_{[[k]]}(\mathbb{Z}_{[[n]]}) \) equals the characteristic of its entry ring \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[n]]} \), since \( kI = 0 \) (the zero matrix) exactly when every entry \( k \cdot 1 \) vanishes in \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[n]]} \), i.e. exactly when \( [[n]] \mid k \). So the characteristic is \( [[answer]] \), independent of the matrix size \( [[k]] \).
distractor_rationales: This multiplies the modulus by the matrix size; the matrix size does not affect the characteristic.
This raises the modulus to the matrix-size power, confusing characteristic with the size of the ring \( |R| = n^{k^2} \).
This uses the matrix dimension itself instead of the entry ring's modulus.
This is one less than the correct characteristic.
This is one more than the correct characteristic.
This is one fewer than the correct characteristic.

stem: Let \( F \) be a finite field with \( [[qval]] = [[p]]^{[[k]]} \) elements. What is the characteristic of \( F \)?
solution: A finite field with \( p^{k} \) elements has characteristic \( p \) (the prime), not \( p^{k} \) itself: it contains a copy of \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[p]]} \) as its prime subfield, and adding \( 1 \) to itself \( [[p]] \) times gives \( 0 \). So the characteristic is \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This confuses the ORDER of the field \( [[qval]] \) with its characteristic; the characteristic is the prime \( p \), not \( p^{k} \).
This uses the exponent \( k \) instead of the prime base \( p \).
This subtracts one from the field's order, unrelated to the characteristic.
This multiplies the prime by the exponent instead of reporting the prime alone.
This is one more than the correct characteristic.
This is one fewer than the correct characteristic.
